subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Cabinet Diversity by President, 1981–2017
stimulus: Source: Georgia Public Broadcasting News, 2017. Bar graphs showing Cabinet Diversity by President from 1981 to 2017, displaying the percentage of women and racial/ethnic minorities serving in the Cabinet under each president (Reagan, G.H.W. Bush, Clinton, G.W. Bush, and Obama).

parts.prompt: Identify the president who had the highest percentage of women and racial/ethnic minorities serving in the Cabinet.
parts.scoring_notes: Only Obama earns the point. The response must name a specific president — not a party or era.

scoring_points.description: Identify the president who had the highest percentage of women and racial/ethnic minorities serving in the Cabinet.

required_elements: President Obama had the highest percentage of women and racial/ethnic minorities serving in his Cabinet.
alternatives.correct_example: President Obama had the highest percentage of women and racial/ethnic minorities serving in his Cabinet.

wrong_examples: Clinton had the highest percentage. (Incorrect — Clinton was second highest, not highest)
Democratic presidents had the highest percentage. (Names a party, not a specific president — does not earn the point)
common_traps: Students sometimes confuse Obama and Clinton — Clinton had a noticeably lower percentage than Obama
Naming a party rather than a specific president does not earn the identification point
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying the president that had the highest percentage of women and racial/ethnic minorities serving in the Cabinet. Acceptable response: President Obama had the highest percentage of women and racial/ethnic minorities serving in his Cabinet.

sample_responses.response: President Obama had the highest percentage of women and racial/ethnic minorities serving in his Cabinet.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies Obama as the president with the highest Cabinet diversity. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Democratic presidents had the most diverse Cabinets.
sample_responses.commentary: Names a party rather than a specific president. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Describe a pattern in the percentage of racial and ethnic minorities serving in Cabinet positions.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate pattern description supported by the data. The response must be a pattern (trend or relationship), not just a single data point.

scoring_points.description: Describe a pattern in the percentage of racial and ethnic minorities serving in Cabinet positions.

required_elements: Democratic presidents tend to appoint more racial and ethnic minorities to Cabinet positions than Republican presidents.
alternatives.correct_example: Democratic presidents tend to appoint more racial and ethnic minorities to Cabinet positions than Republican presidents.

required_elements: Recent presidents are appointing more racial and ethnic minorities to Cabinet positions, regardless of party.
alternatives.correct_example: Recent presidents are appointing more racial and ethnic minorities to Cabinet positions, regardless of party.

wrong_examples: Obama appointed more minorities than Reagan. (Describes a single comparison between two data points, not a broader pattern)
The percentage of minorities in the Cabinet is high. (Makes no pattern claim — just a vague characterization)
common_traps: Identifying a single data point or comparing only two presidents is not a 'pattern' — AP readers require a trend over time or a relationship across groups
Do not confuse women and racial/ethnic minorities — the prompt asks specifically about racial and ethnic minorities
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for describing a pattern in the percentage of racial and ethnic minorities serving in Cabinet positions. Acceptable responses include: Democratic presidents tend to appoint more racial and ethnic minorities to Cabinet positions than Republican presidents; recent presidents are appointing more racial and ethnic minorities to Cabinet positions, regardless of party.

sample_responses.response: Democratic presidents tend to appoint more racial and ethnic minorities to Cabinet positions than Republican presidents.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a partisan pattern across multiple presidents rather than a single data comparison. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Obama appointed more minorities than Reagan.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a single comparison between two data points — not a broader pattern or trend. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion about the political parties and the diversity of Cabinet appointments as shown in the bar graphs.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any plausible conclusion supported by the data that makes an inference beyond mere description. The response must go beyond describing what the data show to drawing a broader conclusion.

scoring_points.description: Draw a conclusion about the political parties and the diversity of Cabinet appointments as shown in the bar graphs.

required_elements: Democratic presidents have appointed more women and minorities because that reflects the demographics of their voters.
alternatives.correct_example: Democratic presidents have appointed more women and minorities because that reflects the demographics of their voters.

required_elements: Republican presidents in the future could appoint more women and minorities to Cabinet level positions in an attempt to appeal to a wider group of voters.
alternatives.correct_example: Republican presidents in the future could appoint more women and minorities to Cabinet level positions in an attempt to appeal to a wider group of voters.

wrong_examples: Democratic presidents appointed more women and minorities than Republican presidents. (Describes the data — does not draw a conclusion about why or what it means)
The data shows that diversity increased over time. (Describes the trend without drawing an inference or explaining its significance)
common_traps: Simply restating a pattern from Part B as the conclusion does not earn this point — the conclusion must go further by explaining why or making a predictive/causal inference
A conclusion must be grounded in the data — purely speculative claims without data support do not earn the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for drawing a conclusion about the political parties and the diversity of Cabinet appointments as shown in the bar graphs. Acceptable responses include: Democratic presidents have appointed more women and minorities because that reflects the demographics of their voters; Republican presidents in the future could appoint more women and minorities to Cabinet level positions in an attempt to appeal to a wider group of voters.

sample_responses.response: Democratic presidents have appointed more women and minorities because that reflects the demographics of their voters.
sample_responses.commentary: Goes beyond describing the data to draw a causal inference about why — connecting appointment patterns to voter demographics. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Democratic presidents appointed more women and minorities than Republican presidents.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the data without drawing a conclusion about why or what it means. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how interest groups can influence presidential Cabinet appointments as shown in the bar graphs.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain a specific mechanism by which interest groups could influence appointments. Vague statements about 'pressuring' the president without describing a specific method do not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Explain how interest groups can influence presidential Cabinet appointments as shown in the bar graphs.

required_elements: Interest groups can use media campaigns to pressure the president to make Cabinet appointments that reflect the interests of the group.
alternatives.correct_example: Interest groups can use media campaigns to pressure the president to make Cabinet appointments that reflect the interests of the group.

required_elements: Interest groups can use media campaigns to pressure the Senate to confirm or not confirm appointments based on how they reflect the interests of the group.
alternatives.correct_example: Interest groups can use media campaigns to pressure the Senate to confirm or not confirm appointments based on how they reflect the interests of the group.

required_elements: Interest groups can lobby members of the Senate to vote for or against a presidential Cabinet nominee.
alternatives.correct_example: Interest groups can lobby members of the Senate to vote for or against a presidential Cabinet nominee.

wrong_examples: Interest groups can pressure the president to appoint diverse Cabinet members. (Too vague — does not name a specific mechanism such as lobbying, campaign contributions, media campaigns, or Senate confirmation pressure)
Interest groups have a lot of power in politics. (Does not explain how they influence Cabinet appointments specifically)
common_traps: The response must name a specific mechanism — general statements about 'pressuring' the president are insufficient
Remember that Cabinet appointments also go through Senate confirmation — interest group influence on Senate confirmation is a valid and often overlooked avenue
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how interest groups can influence presidential Cabinet appointments as shown in the bar graphs. Acceptable responses include: Interest groups can use media campaigns to pressure the president to make Cabinet appointments that reflect the interests of the group; interest groups can use media campaigns to pressure the Senate to confirm or not confirm appointments based on how they reflect the interests of the group; interest groups can lobby members of the Senate to vote for or against a presidential Cabinet nominee.

sample_responses.response: Interest groups can use media campaigns to pressure the president to make Cabinet appointments that reflect the interests of the group.
sample_responses.commentary: Names a specific mechanism (media campaigns) and explains how it influences the president's appointment decisions. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Interest groups can lobby members of the Senate to vote for or against a presidential Cabinet nominee.
sample_responses.commentary: Names lobbying the Senate as a specific mechanism during the confirmation process — a commonly overlooked avenue. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Interest groups have a lot of power and can pressure the president.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — does not name a specific mechanism or strategy. 0 pts.
